sentences.text: Yes.
The interesting-- an interesting thing happened.
When I finally returned to Hannover for the first time, that was in 1947.
I'm sorry, in 1987-- correction. '87.
I was very anxious to visit the Continental Gummi-Werke, but I didn't know how.
This trip was a filming trip for-- for the documentary film that we were working on when I was 14, And I wanted to take this opportunity to see the factory where I labored as a-- as a young girl.
And I asked the crew if they had any ideas, and none came forward.
And I said well, I had in mind just go there and knock on the door and tell them that a long time ago, I worked here and I wanted to see how the place changed.
And of course, they laughed.
But I was perfectly serious.
Not having had a better idea, this was what I was going to do.

But I didn't have to do that, because, at this time, I also had the occasion to-- to speak from a platform to unveil a memorial stone to the Holocaust victims lost at the Continental Gummi-Werke, actually.
And I was honored to do this.
And while we were there, we scouted the place.
And I-- I had certain landmarks in mind.
There were two canals.
And I could just visualize the slave laborers around the-- the banks of the canals.
But we couldn't find them for a while.
But we-- of course, we did, after we looked at an aerial map from Washington, DC.
This aerial map showed that there were no trees along the banks of the canal yet.
But when we returned-- when I returned with the film crew-- there all these trees which prevented-- prevented us from seeing the canal.
And so, of course, we saw the canal.
And at this time, excursion boats were going by.
People were having cocktails.

And I couldn't help but comment how times change, that-- that it just seemed so incongruous, people having such a good time at a place where people were beaten to death, and those helpless victims would fall into the water.
These canals were very, very useful during World War II.
They had them all over, at least Hannover, and I think certain parts of Germany.
This saved the German government a lot of money in energy, because right by this-- all the goods that was produced in the factory was taken by slave laborers to these large barges.
And then they would push them down the canal to its destination and-- not having to use gasoline or car or truck or train or whatever.
So while we were at the-- scouting this place, I asked a historian to come along, a German historian, who was very kind and did so.
Dr.

Stuhlberg from the history department of the University of Hannover, who also invited me to speak at the University, wrote a book, which included his entire history department, but students actually who already worked on their secondary degrees, master's or PhDs.
And so this book, in two volumes, was written about-- about concentration camps in and around Hannover.

And this came about because before the people around Hannover learned that there was a concentration camp, one, And so, just around that time, this neo-Nazi movement started becoming stronger and stronger and historians became concerned.
Well, some historians became concerned where this may lead to, so they decided in Hannover to do something about it.
So the professors decided to start right there, in their own backyard.
And-- and they found that they not only had one concentration camp, but about five concentration camps in Hannover.
Consequently, the book was written based on their findings, and major exhibits were had about it in Hannover, to educate the public about it.
When you were at the Continental Gummi-Werke scouting around, did you get to speak to anyone there?
Ah, thank.
You.
This is where Dr.
Stuhlberg, Claus Stuhlberg comes in.

He said, in the process of writing this book, several people, authors of the book, came and interviewed people who lived in these summer homes, where I thought-- that I thought I belonged to the SS when I was incarcerated, only to find that, no, they were residents.
And they couldn't find anybody who remembered this period.
And while we were walking there, I said, there are some people working in the yard.
I'd like to talk to them.
He said, Gloria, you're wasting your time.
We already thoroughly examined this before.
So I said, if you don't mind, I'd like to ask these people, and then I'll be satisfied.
And it so happened that these-- the lady, Mrs.
Lena, said, well, you're very lucky, because we are the only ones left here.
We are the oldest residents of this area.
And yes, we remember very well.
And so we-- [LAUGHS] she asked us in and we sat down and talked, but we couldn't film her, because it was already beyond dusk.
So she invited us to come the following day.
And we came and we sat in her yard.
And she served us schnapps and cookies.
And I drank to a good neighbor.
Well, she related to me the time of that period, when I was incarcerated at the Continental Gummi-Werke.
She said she was a young girl of about 16.
And they would have parties in their backyard.
And they even have photos that they took from their backyard, looking right into-- right across the electric wire fence, through which everything was quite visible.
And you could see our barracks.
You could see the electric wire fence.
You could see the factory and the logo, Continental.
So-- and she said, at night, we would hear these terrible screams.
What were they, indeed?
And, of course, I explained to her that-- that-- what happened, that the civilians didn't like to see us get beaten on the job.
And some of-- some of us just went to sleep.
We were so exhausted.
Some people just collapsed.
And-- and civilians reacted to the beating of someone so helpless.
So at night, they would be called out, and they would-- they would get the punishment in the barracks.
And, of course, the-- they screamed.
And across the street, where she lived, she would overhear these screams, and 47 years later, relate it to me.
So I-- Why do you think they didn't do anything about it when they heard the screams?
There was nothing she could do about it, she said, because her father, who was a Communist was-- was a Communist and they printed a newspaper, an underground newspaper and-- in this little place.
And her mother had everybody's little Communist book, members book.
I'm sorry, not members of the Communist Party, but the-- the people who subscribed to this little newspaper.
And when-- when the Gestapo came in from the street, they had to come down a couple of steps into the yard and walk a bit to the house.
And her mother noticed them.
And immediately, she ran for the pile of books and she-- she hid it under her garter belt, under her skirt.
And when the Gestapo looked everywhere, they couldn't find a trace of the book.
That's what-- the books, and that's what they were looking for.
Everything else was very well hidden at all times, covered up in a way that it was not possible for them to see it easily.
So-- but her father was, nevertheless, sent to a concentration camp for two years.
And when he came out, he had to go to the Gestapo every week to report.
47 Years before that, we would go into our barracks, and-- hoping to get a good night's sleep and get away from the monsters that we dreamt about.
And instead, we had to meet the punishment.
And we never knew who was going to be punished.
And, of course, as it worked out, we just cried for the other person, because this was-- [CRYING] it was very difficult to see.
I can't talk about that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were all subjected to some of this periodically.
I'd like to move on, however, from Hannover, where I was taken to Hamburg.
In Hamburg, I did, again, street work, helped keep the-- clear the rubble from the major streets.
I remember we were right by the harbor.
And again, there was-- the alarm could be heard very often throughout the day and night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- this meant that the Allies were bombing.
And, of course, wherever we looked, we could see Hamburg destroyed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Once in a while, people would pop up from basements and suddenly they were there.
The streets were all closed form the rubble, as the houses fell onto the streets sometimes.
And we had to, of course, clear this, and push it over to the sidewalk, and then from the sidewalk over to the building, as we did in Braunschweig.
It was the same purpose.
And again, I was there for a limited period of time, maybe about three weeks or four weeks.
I really can't recall accurately.
And then we were shipped out.
And from there, I was sent to a little town called Beendorf.
That's B-E-E-N-D-O-R-F.
Now, it was only something like population 800, a very small town.
But it was a very significant place for-- in Germany, for in the-- in a salt mine, which was so huge that it really ran clear across the town and beyond on both ends.
As I researched, I saw on the map that-- how big it is.
There was a huge salt mountain, white.
And it would shine as we would walk to work toward dusk.
And we'd work during the night and-- we had the night shift-- and come back at about sunrise, to our barrack.
Now, I worked 1,200 feet underground in a gallery.
There were a series of galleries.
And the way-- the place looked like an anthill, an enlarged anthill underground.
Everything was white.
And-- and the streets were narrow, of course, and just long enough for maybe a-- I don't know-- maybe 5 and 1/2 feet or a 6 feet tall person to go by without bending over.
We were-- we-- a huge elevator took us down into the mine shaft.
And from there, we walked into the gallery that was filled with machines.
That-- everything was white.
Everything except the electric wires, the machines, and the human beings that were around.
Everything else was salt, the ceiling, the walls, the floor, and so on.
So these were like rock salt that had to be mined out, which was done by slave laborers during World War II.
It is an old mine, but it was enlarged and widened and-- for World War II-- during World War II.
The streets were called Adolf-Hitler-Strasse, Hermann-Goring-Strasse.
And all the top Nazis received the honor of having one of these streets named after them.
My job was to-- well, my job was to make parts for precision instruments.
Well, these are-- I'm sorry.
These were precision instruments for the V1 and V2 rockets, and these were the rockets used by the Germans during World War II.
I know that the Parliament building in London was hit, and quite a bit of damage was done to it.
And other European capitals were affected by it.
And the Allies feared these most.
Hitler took us out of the camp-- out of Auschwitz to do these jobs important to Germany, in other words, to help with the German war effort.
I was 15 years old at the time.
And I was assigned to work on a machine.
A German man, a civilian man, trained me how to do this.
Now, I was selected to do this job in-- in Hamburg, where industrialists were sent into the camp to hand-pick those who would work in the underground factory.
The way we were picked was by holding our hands up.
I thought that we-- we were being punished at the time.
And I didn't know why, but we got used to that.
There need not be a reason to be punished.
And then, if we had a small hands, then we were picked to do this work.
And having been young at the time, having had small hands, I qualified to work with this small precision instrument.
I learned about this process actually later.
I've talked about this procedure, but I didn't know that this was to be for the work.

So it took me really years afterwards, when I ran into a German person who was working on his PhD and his subject was Hitler's underground factories and I asked-- he wanted to interview me while I was in Germany.
And in return I asked him for a favor, to share some information about it with me.
When I learned that he also did the Beendorf underground factories, I certainly [LAUGHS] raised an ear or an eyebrow.
And-- and I was most grateful to him.
And we are in touch.
So this man who trained me was very kind, very kind to me.
And he sometimes shared part of his lunch with me.
And this was a tremendously courageous act, because the slightest infraction was punishable by a bullet through the head.
It was top secret.
Now, I might add here that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a few years ago when I returned to Germany, to another camp to-- to-- that I haven't talked about yet, I was interviewed by a reporter in Germany.
And it appeared in the German newspapers, apparently, because the person who interviewed me forwarded an article to me-- well, the article to me, as well as a letter from someone in Germany.
This little lady is in her 90s, around the mid-90s.
And she read the story about Beendorf and that the civilian trainer shared his lunch with me.
And she said her husband was a judge in Germany.
And because of his political philosophies, he was taken from his job, and he was sent to Poland.
He didn't like it in Poland, and so they sent him back to Germany.
And he worked at the underground factory on the V1 and V2 missile project.
And he used to come home, she wrote, telling her that-- that-- it is-- it is unbelievable to what extent human beings could be reduced.
They are so emaciated, they're so raggedy, they are so exhausted that they fall asleep on their jobs.
And-- and they don't give them anything to eat to speak of.
And so she was so taken by his description of us, that from then on, she packed an extra lunch to share with somebody.
And she thinks that it was her husband who shared his lunch with me.
Now, I think that's wonderful.
And whether or not it was this lady's husband, I have much feelings for this person and-- and the way he took his life in his own hands to do this.
He just left it there.
He took a bite into his lunch and he just left it there.
And he didn't tell me, but there was sign language through eyes that I may go and eat that which he left there.
So I had to be careful, of course, to eat it discreetly, because I'm not supposed to be eating except what they gave me officially.
So it was a very interesting experience many years later for me.
I also have another little story about-- that-- that is relating-- that relates to this Beendorf.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Many, many years later, I found that my father and my brother Shandor were working only about 60 miles from Beendorf, where I was, in the mother factory, where they dynamited silos and tunnels for the V1 and V2 rockets.
Now, that is where the action was.
That is where they tested these rockets.
And neither of us knew that the other was alive.
And this happened only a few years ago when one day I decided to plot on a map the concentration camps that I was in, and that my father and my brother Shandor were in.
Dora-Mittlebau.
Dora-Mittlebau.
D-O-R-A dash M-I-T-T-L-E-B-A-U.
Yes.
And I'd like to move on to the next camp, because after Beendorf I was sent to Ravensbruck, which was strictly a women's camp.
And it was notorious for its vicious medical experiments.
But, thank God, I was not subjected to any of these cruel experiments.
There was a whole area that was affected by this.
But there in-- in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ravensbruck, there were many, many non-Jewish political prisoners, people who worked in the underground, Danes, French, British.
And they were caught.
And for example, I have a friend in Marin County, who was-- who is a non-Jewish woman and was born in England, married a Frenchmen and lived in Paris.
During the war, she worked for the underground.
She had a little son about six or seven years old.
And she was caught and was sent to Ravensbruck.
And there are many, many people like this.

And I understand that Himmler himself had a sister-- and this is apparently a very little-known story, but I learned this in Germany, actually, that he had a-- has a sister-- or had a sister, definitely had.
And because she had either a Jewish husband or a Jewish boyfriend, which-- whom she did not wish to leave, and so he sent her to Ravensbruck.
So-- and I understand that there were many famous, well-known women, particularly from France, who were incarcerated in Ravensbruck.
There were a lot of artists.
They were very helpful.
I can't remember exactly what I did there, but I do remember this.
We worked in some factory.
And I know that there were a lot of women.
I was there for a very short time and I was in very bad shape by this time, so no wonder that my memory isn't as clear as it-- as it could be.
The SS wanted us to increase our volume.
And they went about it in a very interesting way.
They issued some sort of coupons.

And that if-- they told a group that, if you-- mainly French women-- that if you finish a certain quantity of work, then we will give you certain coupons valued a certain amount and you can go and buy yourselves things with it.
Now, I've never heard this in a camp, in any of the other camps, because Jews were not exposed to this.
I was really one of the very few Jews among us, very few Jews.
There were thousands of us, many thousands.
And so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes.
We were--
Well, they received coupons for having done a lot of work.
I think I need a little more time for that.
Ah.
